title: cmux-reviewer: replace blind background wait with Monitor-based event loop
description:
## Problem

The cmux-reviewer agent (beads-workflow/agents/cmux-reviewer.md) invokes Codex via `codex-companion.mjs adversarial-review --background` and then blindly waits for a callback (line 91: 'wait for Codex to report back automatically'). When Codex hangs in the 'starting' phase (seen live: 25+ minutes stuck in 'starting'), the agent has no way to detect or react — review sessions stall indefinitely.

Current fallback: user notices nothing is happening, cancels manually via `codex-companion cancel <jobId>`, restarts.

## Goal

Make the cmux-reviewer reactive to Codex progress/stall/failure events in real time, so it can cancel+retry on stalls and escalate on hard failures without manual intervention. The cmux-reviewer pane also becomes visibly informative (phase transitions emitted as events).

## Approach

Use the Monitor tool (event-streaming background watcher) instead of blind waiting:

1. Codex still launched with `--background` (gets jobId, returns immediately).
2. Monitor runs a poll script that emits ONE line per meaningful event:
   - PHASE_CHANGE (starting→running, etc.)
   - STALL_DETECTED (>5min in 'starting')
   - CODEX_DONE / CODEX_FAILED / CODEX_CANCELLED (all terminal states — silence is never success)
3. Each emitted line becomes a notification to the agent → real-time reactivity.
4. Monitor `timeout_ms` provides hard cap (~40min).

## Deliverables

1. **New script** beads-workflow/scripts/codex-watch.sh — poll loop with stall detection and terminal-state coverage.
2. **Agent frontmatter** beads-workflow/agents/cmux-reviewer.md — add `Monitor` to `tools:` list.
3. **Phase 2/4 instructions rewrite** in cmux-reviewer.md (lines 68–100, 124–185) — replace 'wait for callback' with Monitor invocation + reaction logic (STALL → cancel+retry once, second STALL → user escalation; FAILED → read log, decide; DONE → continue to Phase 3).
4. **Verify status field names** — run `node codex-companion.mjs status <jobId> --json` against a real job to confirm actual field names (phase / elapsedSeconds / status) before hardcoding them in the watch script.
5. **Test** on a real adversarial-review run against a small bead worktree.
acceptance_criteria:
1. codex-watch.sh exists and emits exactly one line per terminal state (done/failed/cancelled/stalled); silence-on-crash bug impossible.
2. cmux-reviewer.md frontmatter includes Monitor in tools list.
3. Phase 2 + Phase 4 instructions in cmux-reviewer.md no longer contain the phrase 'wait for Codex to report back automatically' or equivalent blind-wait pattern.
4. Agent has explicit reaction logic for each event type (STALL_DETECTED, CODEX_FAILED, CODEX_DONE, CODEX_CANCELLED) documented in the agent markdown.
5. Verified against real codex-companion status --json output — field names in the watch script match reality (not guessed).
6. End-to-end test: launch cmux-reviewer on a real bead worktree, observe event stream in the pane, confirm agent reacts to at least one non-trivial event (phase change or completion).
7. Hard timeout: Monitor timeout_ms set to a value that triggers before the user gets impatient (<=40min) and the agent handles the timeout gracefully.
close_reason: Monitor-based poll loop (codex-watch.sh) replaces blind wait — STALL_DETECTED triggers cancel+retry, CODEX_FAILED reads error log, hard 40min timeout; mktemp dep removed; error reason restored in CODEX_WATCH_ERROR

title: [FEATURE] cld -bw/-bl wave dispatch + -h help
description:
Add wave-orchestrator routing to cld wrapper. Currently cld -b always launches the single-bead orchestrator, even when the target has children. The wave-orchestrator skill exists but has no cld entry point.

Scope:
1. New flag -bw <bead-id>: if bead has children (via bd show --children), launch beads-workflow:wave-orchestrator in a worktree (wave-${id}); else error with guidance.
2. New flag -bl <label[,label2,...]>: query bd list -l <labels> --status=open (AND semantics), launch wave-orchestrator with matched IDs in prompt. Worktree name: wave-${first-label}.
3. Guard on -b: if target bead has children, exit with error pointing to -bw. No worktree created.
4. cld -h / --help: print wrapper flag documentation (-b, -bq, -br, -bp, -bw, -bl, -c, -v, --skip-perms, --force-check, --no-check), then fall through to claude --help.

Design decisions:
- Children only, never dep-edges. 'Has children' == bd show --children non-empty.
- AND semantics for -bl labels (matches bd list -l behavior).
- Separate flags -bw and -bl (explicit > magic sigil).
- Drop epic-type concept entirely.
acceptance_criteria:
1. cld -bw <id> with children launches wave-orchestrator in worktree wave-${id}
2. cld -bw <id> without children exits non-zero with error referencing -b
3. cld -bl label launches wave-orchestrator with beads matching bd list -l label --status=open
4. cld -bl label1,label2 uses AND semantics (both labels required)
5. cld -b <id> where id has children exits non-zero with error referencing -bw
6. cld -h and cld --help show wrapper flags before falling through
7. -bl worktree uses first label (sanitized) as name
8. No regression on existing -b, -bq, -br, -bp, -c, -v flags
close_reason: Implemented in ~/.claude@fcc16e8: -bw, -bl, -b epic guard, -h help. Verified: epic guard rejects parent beads with exit 2; -bw rejects leaf beads with exit 2; -bl rejects no-match labels with exit 2; --help shows wrapper flags then claude --help.

title: bead-orchestrator Phase 2.6 — Module-Impact-Analyse als Pflichtschritt
description:
Erweitert bead-orchestrator um einen expliziten Phase-2.6-Schritt VOR Implementation: Der Subagent wird aufgefordert, die Module zu identifizieren, die er aendern wird, UND die existierenden Patterns in diesen Modulen zu scannen.

Ziel: Verhindern, dass Implementer Pattern-inkonsistent arbeitet (z.B. neuen Logging-Stil einfuehrt, obwohl das Projekt schon einen hat).

Mechanik: Nach dem Architecture-Context-Block (aus CCP-1ul) kommt ein 'Module Impact Analysis' Schritt: Implementer listet betroffene Module, Grep nach 3-5 vorhandenen Patterns in diesen Modulen, stellt diese in den Prompt, dann erst Code.

Kostet ~30 Sekunden pro Bead, verhindert Stunden Rework. Voraussetzung: CCP-1ul muss fertig sein.

Subagent-Prompt enthaelt 'Existing Patterns' Block mit 3-5 Grep-Auszuegen aus diesen Modulen. 4. Test: Ein Feature-Bead wird implementiert, der resultierende Code folgt den identifizierten Patterns (kein Random-neuer-Stil).

Sub-ms latency. Complements rtk (already installed for token compression). Use the 'if:' hook field so DCG only fires on dangerous patterns. Hook order: DCG first (block), rtk second (compress).

title: [FEAT] Wave-1: Model-Routing + metrics.db Erweiterung
description:
## Ziel

Implementiere die Model-Strategie aus CCP-25m Wave-1: Feste Model-Zuweisung pro Workflow-Phase + Tracking in metrics.db.

## Scope

### 1. Config-Datei (.claude/model-strategy.yml)

Feste Model-Zuweisung pro Phase:

```yaml
phase1:  # Bead Orchestrator Loop
  orchestrator: opus
  implementation: codex
  review: opus

phase2:  # CMUX Reviewer Loop
  reviewer: opus
  adversarial: codex
  fix: sonnet
  fix_review: codex  # Codex reviewed Sonnet-Fixes nochmal adversarial
```

Keine CLI-Flags, keine dynamische Auswahl.

### 2. Strukturiertes Codex Briefing-Template

Opus (Orchestrator) schreibt Impl-Anweisungen in festem Format für Codex:

```markdown
## Ziel
[Was soll implementiert werden und warum]

## Dateien
[Welche Dateien betroffen sind, mit Pfaden]

## TDD-Plan
### Red (Tests schreiben)
[Welche Tests, welche Assertions]
### Green (Implementation)
[Wie implementieren, welche Patterns]
### Refactor
[Was aufräumen]

## Injections
[Standard-Injections: Standards, Patterns, Constraints]

## Constraints
[Was NICHT tun, Scope-Grenzen]
```

### 3. Bead Orchestrator anpassen

- Orchestrator liest model-strategy.yml
- Orchestrator (Opus) schreibt strukturiertes Briefing für Codex
- Implementation Agent wird mit Codex gestartet (statt Sonnet)
- Review Agent bleibt Opus
- Verification Agent bleibt Sonnet (vorerst)
- Loop-Logik unverändert

### 4. CMUX Reviewer anpassen

- Adversarial Review bleibt Codex (bereits so)
- Fix Implementation wechselt auf Sonnet (statt Codex) — bewusstes Umdrehen
- Nach Sonnet-Fix: Codex reviewed nochmal adversarial (Fix-Quality-Check)
- CMUX Reviewer Agent bleibt Opus

### 5. metrics.db Schema erweitern

Neue Spalten in bead_runs:
- wave_id TEXT — welche Wave hat dieses Bead dispatched
- model_impl TEXT — welches Model hat implementiert (codex/sonnet)
- model_review TEXT — welches Model hat reviewed (opus/sonnet)
- phase2_triggered BOOLEAN — wurde CMUX adversarial Review ausgelöst
- phase2_findings INTEGER — Anzahl Findings aus Phase 2
- phase2_critical INTEGER — davon CRITICAL

Bead-Orchestrator schreibt diese Felder in Phase 3.6 (Token Capture).
Wave-Orchestrator setzt wave_id beim Dispatch.

### 6. Bead-Notes Audit

Model-Assignment wird zusätzlich in Bead-Notes festgehalten (minimal audit).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] model-strategy.yml existiert und wird von Orchestrator + CMUX Reviewer gelesen
- [ ] Codex Briefing-Template ist im Bead Orchestrator implementiert
- [ ] Implementation Agent startet mit Codex (verifizierbar in Bead-Notes/Logs)
- [ ] Review Agent nutzt Opus (verifizierbar)
- [ ] Verification Agent bleibt Sonnet
- [ ] Fix Implementation in Phase 2 nutzt Sonnet (nicht Codex)
- [ ] Codex reviewed Sonnet-Fixes in Phase 2 nochmal adversarial
- [ ] metrics.db hat neue Spalten (ALTER TABLE, abwärtskompatibel)
- [ ] Nach einem Bead-Run: model_impl, model_review, phase2_triggered in metrics.db korrekt befüllt
- [ ] wave_id wird vom Wave-Orchestrator gesetzt und in metrics.db geschrieben
- [ ] Bestehende bead-metrics Skill Queries funktionieren weiterhin (Regression)
close_reason: Implemented: model-strategy.yml, metrics.db extension (6 cols + migration), bead-orchestrator (Opus + Codex briefing), cmux-reviewer (Phase 2 metrics), wave-dispatch (wave_id), bead-metrics (wave queries). Pushed as v2026.04.9.

title: [FEAT] Test quality gate: enforce mock-interface consistency and test isolation in review pipeline
description:
## Problem

On 2026-04-11, we shipped 4 beads that were purely test-quality repairs (bslz/3gw7/1g1c/sgu3).
These arose because feature beads (zcas, 0pu5, ckul, j2wq) were merged with test gaps:

1. **Mock type drift** (0pu5): \`SyncRunStore.insert()\` changed from \`void\` to \`Promise<void>\`,
   but 5 test files still had \`insert: () => {}\` instead of \`insert: () => Promise.resolve()\`.
   The bead-orchestrator's internal review and verification agent both passed this because
   \`tsc --noEmit\` doesn't catch mock objects (they use \`as unknown as ...\` casts).

2. **mock.module leaks** (sgu3): Tests in count-by-scope.test.ts used \`mock.module()\` which
   doesn't clean up between tests in Bun. The feature beads that added these mocks (ckul, fxz0)
   didn't test for cross-test pollution.

3. **Async test pollution** (sgu3): sync-route.test.ts fires background jobs that race with
   subsequent tests. The feature beads that modified handleSyncStart didn't add drain logic.

## Current Quality Gates and Where They Miss

### Bead Orchestrator (Phase 3: Internal Review — 3 iterations)
- **review-agent** checks: code quality, ACs met, test coverage
- **MISSES**: Mock objects are not type-checked (cast via \`as unknown as ...\`)
- **MISSES**: Cross-file test isolation (one test polluting another)
- **MISSES**: Async test pollution (background jobs escaping test boundaries)

### Bead Orchestrator (Phase 4: Verification Agent)
- Runs \`bun run test\` and \`tsc --noEmit\`
- **MISSES**: Pre-existing failures mask new ones (48 failures → still 48 → "no regression")
- **MISSES**: Test-internal assertions (mock shape vs interface shape)

### cmux-Reviewer (Adversarial Review)
- Checks code quality via Codex
- **MISSES**: Doesn't run tests itself (read-only review)
- **CATCHES**: Sometimes finds mock issues but only by reading code, not by running tests

## Proposed Enhancements

### Enhancement 1: Mock Consistency Gate (in review-agent)

After implementation, before review, run:

\`\`\`bash
# Find all interface changes in the diff
git diff --name-only | grep -E "types\\.ts|store\\.ts|interface" | while read f; do
  # For each changed interface, find all mocks of that interface
  INTERFACE_NAME=$(grep -oP "export (interface|type) \\K\\w+" "$f")
  grep -rn "$INTERFACE_NAME" --include="*.test.ts" | grep -E "mock|Mock|stub|Stub"
done
```

If interface changes are found AND mock implementations exist, the review-agent should
verify that mock shapes match the updated interface.

**Concrete rule for CLAUDE.md** (already added today but can be automated):
"After Interface-Change: grep for all mock implementations, verify return types match."

### Enhancement 2: Test Isolation Check (in verification-agent)

After \`bun run test\` passes, run the affected test files in isolation:

\`\`\`bash
# Run each changed test file individually
for f in $(git diff --name-only | grep "\.test\.ts$"); do
  bun test "$f" 2>&1
done
```

If a test passes in the full suite but fails in isolation (or vice versa), flag it as
a test isolation issue.

### Enhancement 3: Failure Baseline Tracking

Currently the verification agent sees "48 fail" and says "pre-existing". But we should
track the baseline:

```bash
# Before implementation:
bun run test 2>&1 | tail -1 > /tmp/test-baseline.txt
# After implementation:
bun run test 2>&1 | tail -1 > /tmp/test-current.txt
# Compare: new failures = current - baseline
```

If the failure count INCREASES, that's a regression even if the total is still "48 fail".
If the failure count DECREASES, that's a bonus (the bead fixed pre-existing issues).

### Enhancement 4: Async Test Pollution Detection

For test files that import modules with background jobs (\`setTimeout\`, \`setInterval\`,
\`Promise\` without await, fire-and-forget patterns):

```bash
# Check for background job patterns in changed handler code
grep -n "\.then(" "\.catch(" "setTimeout" "setInterval" src/routes/
# If found: verify corresponding test has drain/cleanup in afterEach/afterAll
```

## Where to Insert These Gates

| Gate | Stage | Agent | Implementation |
|------|-------|-------|----------------|
| Mock consistency | Phase 3 (internal review) | review-agent | Add to review checklist |
| Test isolation | Phase 4 (verification) | verification-agent | Run tests in isolation |
| Failure baseline | Phase 4 (verification) | verification-agent | Track pre/post counts |
| Async pollution | Phase 3 (internal review) | review-agent | Pattern detection |

## Impact Estimate

If these gates had existed today, we would have caught:
- 0pu5 mock drift → prevented 3gw7/1g1c/bslz follow-up beads (3 beads saved)
- sgu3 mock.module leak → prevented in the original ckul/fxz0 beads (1 bead saved)
- sgu3 async pollution → prevented in the original 0pu5/dknc beads (1 bead saved)

5 follow-up beads = ~5 hours of compute + review. The gates cost ~30 seconds per bead.

## References

- Verification agent: \`beads-workflow/agents/verification-agent.md\`
- Review agent: \`beads-workflow/agents/review-agent.md\`
- mira-adapters packages/pvs-x-isynet/CLAUDE.md: Now includes mock consistency rule
- sgu3 session data: 120+ tool uses, 3 iterations for test-only fixes

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Mock consistency gate added to review-agent: detects interface→mock drift
- [ ] Test isolation check added to verification-agent: runs changed test files in isolation
- [ ] Failure baseline tracking: compares pre/post failure counts
- [ ] Async pollution detection: flags background jobs without drain/cleanup in tests
- [ ] Gates are lightweight (< 30s overhead per bead)
- [ ] Gates produce clear, actionable error messages (not just "test failed")
close_reason: Implemented in commit 48c0fe2

title: [FEAT] Multi-model orchestration strategy: Codex/Opus/Sonnet role assignment across workflow stages
description:
## Context

Basierend auf Erfahrungen vom 2026-04-11: Codex adversarial Reviews finden systematisch andere (oft wertvollere) Schwachstellen als Opus/Sonnet allein. Model-Diversity ist der Schlüssel — nicht ein einzelnes "bestes" Model.

## Model-Strategie (Council-Review validiert, 2026-04-12)

### Phase 1: Bead Orchestrator Loop

| Rolle | Model | Warum |
|-------|-------|-------|
| Bead Orchestrator | Opus | Sammelt Kontext, schreibt detaillierte Impl-Anweisungen (TDD-Strategie, Injections, Red-Green-Plan) |
| Implementation Agent | Codex | Folgt Anweisungen präzise, macht TDD |
| Review Agent | Opus | Code Review der Codex-Arbeit |
| → Loop bis Orchestrator "passt" sagt | | |

### Phase 2: CMUX Reviewer Loop (bewusst umgedreht)

| Rolle | Model | Warum |
|-------|-------|-------|
| CMUX Reviewer | Opus | Orchestriert den adversarial Review |
| Adversarial Review | Codex | Reviewed gesamten Worktree adversarial |
| Fix Implementation | Sonnet (nicht Codex!) | Bewusstes Umdrehen — anderes Model fixt |

### Kernprinzip

Model-Diversity als Qualitätsmechanismus: Anthropic und OpenAI sind unterschiedlich trainiert und finden unterschiedliche Schwächen. In Phase 1 implementiert Codex und Opus reviewed. In Phase 2 wird umgedreht: Codex reviewed adversarial und Sonnet fixt. Kein Model ist "besser" — die Kombination findet mehr.

Langfristziel: Phase 2 wird überflüssig, wenn Phase-1-Qualität gut genug ist.

## Technische Entscheidungen (Council-Review)

- **Config**: Feste YAML-Config (z.B. .claude/model-strategy.yml), keine CLI-Flags. Eliminiert Injection-Risiko.
- **Tracking**: metrics.db erweitern (bead-getrieben). Neue Spalten: wave_id, model_impl, model_review, phase2_triggered, phase2_findings, phase2_critical.
- **Audit**: Minimal — Model-Assignment in Bead-Notes. Internes Tool, kein formaler Audit nötig.
- **ROI-Metriken**: Follow-up-Beads pro Wave + Fix:Feat-Commit-Ratio.
- **Evaluierung**: Bei jedem Wave-Close Metriken prüfen. Nach 3 Waves ohne CRITICAL Phase-2-Findings → Hinweis an User (nicht auto-deaktivieren).
- **Option C (Adaptive)**: Gestrichen. Feste Strategie statt automatischer Model-Wahl.

## Observations (Evidenz)

- Codex fand td66 Type-Bug (smallint statt nullable), sgu3 silent-catch Anti-Pattern, del8 fehlenden Scope
- Opus fand 0pu5 Pool-Boundary Architektur-Flaw
- Sonnet: schnell aber oberflächlich bei komplexen Design-Entscheidungen
- 84 Fix-Commits vs 21 Feat-Commits = 4:1 Rework-Ratio (Baseline)
- Adversarial Review allein hat architektonische Schwachstellen aufgedeckt die sonst in Produktion gegangen wären
notes:
## Council-Review Entscheidungen (2026-04-12)

### Alle Findings adressiert (8 CRITICAL, 8 WARNING, 4 NOTE → 0 offen)

**Zusätzliche Entscheidungen aus WARNING/NOTE Review:**

1. **Phase-2 Deaktivierung** (#9): Manuell im Skill-Code. Kein Config-Flag, kein technisches Gate. Wenn Phase 2 nicht mehr nötig → User ändert cmux-reviewer Skill direkt.

2. **Sonnet-Fix-Qualität** (#12): Codex reviewed die Sonnet-Fixes in Phase 2 nochmal adversarial. D.h. Phase-2-Loop ist: Codex adversarial → Sonnet fixt → Codex reviewed Fix. Maximale Absicherung durch Model-Wechsel.

3. **Codex Briefing-Format** (#16): Strukturiertes Briefing-Template für Opus→Codex Übergabe. Format: ## Ziel, ## Dateien, ## TDD-Plan (Red→Green→Refactor), ## Injections, ## Constraints. Passt zu Codex-Stärke (folgt strukturierten Anweisungen präzise).

4. **Verification Agent** (#17): Bleibt vorerst (Sonnet). Prüft ob Tests laufen und ACs erfüllt sind — andere Aufgabe als Code Review. Kostet wenig Token. Kann später gestrichen werden wenn Daten zeigen dass er nichts findet.

5. **Note #19 Developer vs. Goal Konflikt**: Entscheidung: Quality > Token-Ersparnis. Verification bleibt, Phase 2 bleibt. Token-Kosten werden akzeptiert solange Qualitätsgewinn messbar ist.

title: [FEAT] Pre-wave architecture review: design-doc gate for complex beads
description:
## Problem

Komplexe architektonische Beads (wie 0pu5 mit 4 Review-Iterationen, cyci pending) brauchen
zu viele Review-Zyklen, weil Design-Entscheidungen nicht VOR Implementation geklärt werden.

## Lösung: Architecture Council Gate (Phase 1.5b)

Der Wave Orchestrator bekommt eine neue Phase 1.5b zwischen Scope-Check (1.5a) und
Scenario-Check (Phase 2). In dieser Phase werden Beads mit architektonischer Komplexität
automatisch erkannt und durch einen Architecture Council reviewed.

### Council-Entscheidungen (aus Council-Review vom 2026-04-12)

**Architektur (vereinfacht vs. Original)**:
- Original hatte 3 Optionen (A: Plan-Agent, B: Council, C: Codex) → **Nur Architecture Council**
- Wave Orchestrator entscheidet on/off (binäre Entscheidung, kein A/B/C)
- Architecture Council nutzt bestehenden /council Skill mit neuem 'architecture' Rollen-Profil
- Model: Opus (nicht Haiku wie beim Standard-Council)
- Kein Codex für Architektur-Reviews

**Signal Detection (Hybrid: Regex + Haiku Fallback)**:
- Strong signals (Regex): title contains [ARCH], description matches /state.machine|boundary|protocol|API.contract/, bd dep count >= 2 in current wave, changed files span 3+ packages
- Medium signals (Regex): title contains [REFACTOR] AND touches core infrastructure, blocks 2+ beads
- Bei unklaren Fällen: Haiku-Agent klassifiziert REVIEW_YES/REVIEW_NO
- Score >= 6 → Review triggern

**Escape Hatch**:
- --skip-review Flag im Wave Orchestrator
- Audit-Log-Eintrag (wer, wann, warum geskippt)
- Break-Glass für Production-Hotfixes mit Owner-Approval

**Design Doc Storage**:
- Temporär im Worktree (NICHT in Git committed)
- Wird beim Session Close mit Worktree aufgeräumt
- Wenn grundsätzliche Architekturentscheidungen fallen → ADRs als separate Beads
- Kein bd update --design (existiert nicht, wird nicht gebaut)

**Timing**: Sequenziell: Phase 1.5a (Scope-Check) → Phase 1.5b (Arch-Review)
**Latenz**: Parallele Councils pro Bead (nicht sequenziell über alle Beads)
**UI/UX**: Inline in Wave-Übersicht, blockierte Beads markiert, COUNCIL_BLOCKED sichtbar
**Baseline**: Review-Iterationen + Token-Kosten der letzten 10-20 Beads VOR Aktivierung erfassen

## Integration mit CCP-80r (Model-Routing)

CCP-80r hat model-strategy.yml eingeführt. Neuer Eintrag:

```yaml
architecture_review:
  council: opus   # Architecture Council Agents laufen auf Opus
```

metrics.db hat bereits wave_id, phase2_findings — Baseline-Daten extrahierbar.
wave-dispatch.sh hat wave_id Tracking — Vorher/Nachher-Messung über wave_id möglich.

title: [FEAT] Wave Orchestrator: Post-wave completion report with reviewer + implementer learnings
description:
## Problem

After Wave 9 on 2026-04-11, the user asked "was haben wir gelernt?" and "wo müssen wir
nachschärfen?" — I had to manually reconstruct findings from memory. The wave orchestrator
currently tracks dispatch and completion status but does NOT aggregate learnings from
individual beads.

With the introduction of Codex adversarial reviews (via cmux-reviewer), we're getting
significantly richer and more diverse feedback than before:
- Codex finds architectural smells that Opus/Sonnet reviewers miss
- Cross-model review produces findings from different "schools of thought"
- The cmux-reviewer generates structured FINDINGS reports with ADVISORY/BLOCKING classification
- The bead-orchestrator generates verification reports and review loop summaries

**All of this is lost** after the surfaces close. Session-close captures some learnings
via open-brain, but the cmux-reviewer does NOT run session-close (by design — it's a
read-only reviewer). Its findings only exist in the surface scrollback.

## What the Wave Orchestrator should do (Phase 7: Completion Report)

After all beads in a wave are closed, before moving to the next wave:

### Data Collection (per bead)
1. **Bead notes** (\`bd show <id>\`): Close reason, review loop summary, iteration count, quality grade
2. **Reviewer findings**: Read the reviewer surface scrollback (before it's cleaned up) and extract:
   - BLOCKING findings (what was fixed)
   - ADVISORY findings (what was deferred → follow-up beads)
   - Codex adversarial findings (if codex was used)
   - Oscillation patterns (what kept bouncing between iterations)
3. **Implementation commits**: \`git log --oneline\` for the bead's branch → what actually changed
4. **Follow-up beads created**: Any beads with \`discovered-from: <bead-id>\`
5. **Time metrics**: Dispatch time → close time, iteration count, total tool uses

### Report Structure
```markdown
## Wave N Completion Report

### Summary
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Beads dispatched | 6 |
| Beads closed | 6 |
| Total iterations across all beads | 14 |
| Follow-up beads created | 4 |
| SCOPE-EXPAND decisions | 1 |
| Duration | ~90 min |

### Per-Bead Summary
| Bead | Iterations | Grade | Key Learning |
|------|-----------|-------|-------------|
| fsw3 | 2 | A | @ts-expect-error format convention needed |
| sgu3 | 3+expand | A- | mock.module leaks need DI pattern |

### Key Learnings (aggregated)
1. **Type safety**: 3 beads (fsw3, td66, del8) all stemmed from pvs-db-types.ts drift
   → Rule added to CLAUDE.md, further beads for full reconciliation
2. **Test isolation**: sgu3 revealed mock.module is file-scoped in Bun, not test-scoped
   → Follow-up bead hu5u for DI refactor
3. **Error semantics**: fxz0 discovered isProbeFatal allowlist pattern after 5 iterations
   → Rule added to CLAUDE.md

### CLAUDE.md Updates Made
- packages/pvs-x-isynet/CLAUDE.md: Type safety, error semantics, pool boundary rules

### Codex Adversarial Findings (highlights)
- td66: RechFormArt type drift was string→number, not just nullable mismatch
- del8: Found EBM2000StammRow and EinwilligungserklaerungRow drift (not in original scope)
- sgu3: Identified silent .catch(() => {}) as anti-pattern

### Follow-up Beads Created
| Bead | Priority | Origin | What |
|------|----------|--------|------|
| hu5u | P2 | sgu3 iter 3 | DI refactor for mock.module |
| gqqq | P2 | sgu3 iter 3 | drainBackgroundSync timeout |
| vv6p | P3 | aezd iter 3 | Hard-coded port in CLI test |
```

### Where to Store the Report
1. **User output**: Display the summary table in the wave orchestrator's main conversation
2. **open-brain**: Save as a memory via \`mcp__open-brain__save_memory\` with tags
   "wave-report", "learnings", project name
3. **bd notes**: Append a summary to each closed bead's notes referencing the wave report
4. **File**: Optionally write to \`docs/wave-reports/YYYY-MM-DD-wave-N.md\` in the project

### Implementation Notes

- The report must be generated BEFORE surfaces are cleaned up (reviewer scrollback
  is the primary data source for findings)
- Use \`cmux read-screen --surface <N> --scrollback --lines 100\` to capture reviewer output
- Parse reviewer output for structured sections: "Summary", "Findings", "Advisory",
  "Follow-up beads recommended"
- For Codex findings: look for "[codex]" prefixed lines in the reviewer scrollback

## Why This Matters

Today we produced 20+ beads across 9 waves. Each bead generated valuable architectural
insights. Without a structured completion report, these insights are:
- Scattered across surface scrollbacks that get destroyed
- Partially in bd notes (bead-orchestrator side only)
- Partially in open-brain (session-close memories — but cmux-reviewer doesn't session-close)
- Mostly in the wave orchestrator's working memory (lost on context compaction)

The completion report turns operational data into institutional knowledge.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Wave orchestrator generates a completion report after each wave
- [ ] Report includes per-bead summary with iterations, grade, key learning
- [ ] Report aggregates Codex adversarial findings from reviewer surfaces
- [ ] Report lists follow-up beads created during the wave
- [ ] Report is saved to open-brain as a memory
- [ ] Report is displayed to the user as a readable table
notes:
## Erkenntnisse aus dem ersten manuellen Report (Wave 9, 2026-04-11)

### Was im Report stehen MUSS (nicht "was gemacht wurde" sondern "was gelernt wurde")

Der erste manuelle Report-Versuch beschrieb nur Commits und Completion-Status. Der User
wies darauf hin dass die eigentlich wertvollen Inhalte die **Reviewer-Findings** sind:

1. **Architektur-Erkenntnisse**: Was war vorher unsichtbar und ist jetzt durch die
   Review aufgetaucht? (z.B. adapter-common divergiert von pvs-x-isynet Mapping-Annahmen,
   silent catch { return [] } als Observability-Blindspot)

2. **Codex-spezifische Findings**: Codex findet ANDERE Dinge als Opus/Sonnet (branded
   nominal types, NOT NULL tightening, Schema-to-Type Generator als Wurzel-Lösung).
   Diese Findings sind oft die wertvollsten — sie zeigen systemische Probleme.

3. **Prozess-Learnings**: Wo hat das Impl↔Reviewer Ping-Pong Fehler gefangen?
   Wo hat SCOPE-EXPAND als bewusste Entscheidung funktioniert?

4. **Konkrete Bead-Empfehlungen**: Aus den Review-Findings destillierte nächste Schritte
   mit geschätztem Aufwand.

### Report-Struktur (korrigiert)

```
## Wave N — Learnings Report

### Architektur-Erkenntnisse
[Nummeriert, jeweils mit: Was war vorher unsichtbar? Warum ist es jetzt sichtbar?
Was ist die Konsequenz? Wer hat es gefunden (Codex/Opus/Impl)?]

### Prozess-Erkenntnisse
[Wo hat der Multi-Agent-Workflow etwas gefangen das sonst durchgerutscht wäre?
Wo hat er unnötig Arbeit erzeugt?]

### Konkrete Bead-Empfehlungen aus Reviews
[Tabelle: Quelle | Empfehlung | Aufwand-Schätzung]

### Completion Table (nachrangig)
[Per-Bead: Iterations, Grade, Key Finding — nur als Referenz]
```

### Datenquelle: cmux-reviewer Scrollback

Die wichtigsten Daten stehen im cmux-reviewer Scrollback:
- Structured Findings Table (BLOCKING/ADVISORY/REGRESSION)
- Codex raw output (DECIDE items, PRE_EXISTING flags)
- Review Meta (oscillation check, iteration ladder)
- "Actionable Improvement Suggestions" Sektion
- "Recommendation for user" pro Finding

KRITISCH: Der Scrollback muss BEVOR die Panes geschlossen werden gelesen werden.
Im Wave Orchestrator: nach wave-completion.sh exit 0, aber VOR Surface-Cleanup,
alle Reviewer-Surfaces lesen (cmux read-screen --scrollback --lines 100).

### Idee: cmux-reviewer session-close "light"

Anstatt den Wave Orchestrator die Findings post-hoc zu extrahieren, könnte der
cmux-reviewer SELBST beim Triggern von session-close seine Findings als open-brain
Memory speichern. Vorteile:

1. Reviewer hat den vollen Kontext seiner eigenen Findings im Fenster
2. Kein Timing-Problem (muss nicht warten bis Wave Orchestrator liest)
3. Findings sind sofort als Memory verfügbar (nicht nur in Surface-Scrollback)
4. Codex-Findings werden mitgesichert (die sonst verloren gehen)

Implementierung im cmux-reviewer.md Agent:
```
Vor dem `cmux send "session close"` an die impl-surface:
1. Sammle alle Findings aus der aktuellen Review-Session
2. Speichere via mcp__open-brain__save_memory:
   - namespace: "wave-review"
   - tags: [bead-id, project, "review-finding"]
   - content: structured findings + learnings
3. DANN erst session-close triggern
```

Das ist ein "session-close light" — nur die Memory-Speicherung, kein Git/Tag/Merge.

title: [FEAT] Wave Orchestrator: Bead scope analysis and auto-split before dispatch
description:
## Problem (observed 2026-04-11)

Three beads today suffered from review oscillation because their scope covered
multiple orthogonal problems:

1. **mira-adapters-0pu5** (4 iterations): "SyncRunStore.insert() awaitable + error propagation"
   combined pool boundary changes + error semantics + test fixture updates. Each fix
   introduced new issues in the other dimension.

2. **mira-adapters-fxz0** (5 iterations): "ProbeFailedError returns 503" combined
   error classification logic + route-level error handling + test setup. The isProbeFatal
   allowlist approach was discovered in iteration 4 after trying 3 other patterns.

3. **mira-adapters-sgu3** (3 iterations + SCOPE-EXPAND): "48 pre-existing unit test failures"
   covered 4 completely independent failure categories (async pollution, mock.module leak,
   getBetriebCondition null, shared-adapter). Each category required different fix strategies.
   Fixing category A introduced regressions in category C.

**Pattern**: Beads with compound titles ("X + Y", "X and Y", comma-separated concerns) or
acceptance criteria spanning multiple independent dimensions reliably oscillate in review.

## What the Wave Orchestrator should do (Phase 1.5: Scope Check)

After Phase 1 (dependency sort) and before Phase 2 (scenario check), the wave orchestrator
should analyze each bead's scope:

### Scope Signals
1. **Title analysis**: Does the title contain "and", "+", commas, or multiple verb phrases?
2. **Acceptance criteria independence**: Can each AC be satisfied without affecting the others?
   If AC-1 (fix insert) can be done without touching the code that satisfies AC-4 (fix pool),
   they're independent → should be separate beads.
3. **File overlap**: Read the bead description, identify expected files. If a bead touches
   files in 3+ unrelated directories/modules, it's likely compound.
4. **Historical pattern**: Check if similar beads in the project previously oscillated
   (bd notes contain "iteration 3" or "SCOPE-EXPAND").

### Suggested Implementation

```
For each bead in the wave:
  1. Run bd show <id> and extract title, description, ACs
  2. Spawn a haiku-class subagent with the prompt:
     "Analyze this bead for scope. Can the acceptance criteria be satisfied independently?
      If the bead covers 2+ orthogonal concerns, suggest a split."
  3. If the subagent recommends a split:
     a. Present the split to the user
     b. If approved: create the sub-beads via bd create, mark the parent as superseded
     c. If rejected: proceed with the original bead but add a note "Compound scope — risk of oscillation"
```

### Design Decision: Split or Annotate?

- **Split** (preferred): Create N smaller beads, each with 1-2 ACs that form a coherent unit.
  Dependency edges between the sub-beads if needed.
- **Annotate** (fallback): If the user doesn't want to split, add a note to the bead that
  warns the bead-orchestrator about oscillation risk. The orchestrator can then use a
  SCOPE-EXPAND strategy preemptively (merge after 2 iterations instead of 3).

## Why the Wave Orchestrator should own this (not the Bead Orchestrator)

The wave orchestrator has the bird's-eye view across all beads. It can:
- See which beads might conflict on the same files
- Assess whether splitting one bead creates dependency on another
- Batch the analysis for all beads before dispatching any

The bead orchestrator sees only its own bead and can't coordinate splits across the wave.

## References

- Wave Orchestrator skill: \`beads-workflow/skills/wave-orchestrator/SKILL.md\`
- Bead Orchestrator agent: \`beads-workflow/agents/bead-orchestrator.md\`
- Session context from 2026-04-11: 0pu5 had 4 review iterations, fxz0 had 5, sgu3 had 3+SCOPE-EXPAND

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Wave Orchestrator Phase 1.5 implemented: scope analysis before dispatch
- [ ] Compound beads are detected by title/AC analysis
- [ ] Split recommendations are presented to the user for approval
- [ ] Beads that the user chooses not to split get an oscillation warning annotation
- [ ] Test: Create a compound bead, run wave orchestrator, verify it suggests a split

title: Constitution drift report: vision.md vs project-context.md mismatch detection
description:
Detect drift between the prescriptive constitution (vision.md, hand-written) and the descriptive constitution (project-context.md, auto-generated). Surface mismatches as DECIDE items so vision/code can be reconciled deliberately.

**Why separate from CCP-k1u:**
- Different cadence: post-regeneration (or scheduled patrol), not per-bead
- Different consumer: human reviewer, not orchestrator gate
- Shares rules engine with k1u but trigger is different

**Mechanism:**
- Compare LOCKED sections of vision.md (boundary table, layers list, future-contract section) against current project-context.md (module map, detected patterns)
- Report mismatches in two directions:
  - **Vision is behind**: project-context shows a module/pattern that vision doesn't acknowledge (e.g. packages/agent-api/ exists but vision §4 still has it as planned)
  - **Code is behind**: vision declares a module/contract that project-context doesn't show (e.g. packages/cognovis-install-pvs/ in vision §5 but missing from filesystem)

**Surface as:**
- New skill /constitution-drift (or /project-context --drift flag)
- Output: structured DECIDE list — each mismatch + suggested resolution (update vision OR file bead to make code match)
- Optional: scheduled patrol via cron skill (weekly drift report)

**Acceptance criteria:**
- [ ] /constitution-drift (or equivalent) command exists
- [ ] Reads vision.md locked sections and project-context.md current state
- [ ] Outputs both directions of drift (vision-behind, code-behind)
- [ ] Suggested resolution per mismatch (update doc | file bead)
- [ ] Smoke test: introduce a fake drift, command detects it

**Depends on:**
- CCP-u87 (need named/parseable Reading Corpus section to know which sections to lock)
- CCP-k1u (shares rules engine — pull common code into a shared utility)
acceptance_criteria: Drift command detects vision↔project-context mismatches, surfaces both directions, suggests resolutions, smoke-tested
notes: Suggested as 'fourth child' by mira-agent review of CCP-w56 epic, 2026-04-19. Could fold into k1u if it stays small (<200 LOC), but separate file/skill keeps the cadence/consumer split clean.
close_reason: Superseded by Trinity-Harness CCP-wxv (review-agent Contract-Drift-Phase). Folded in as a 5th heuristic ('code violates a stated vision principle'). The 'vision-behind-code' case that justified a separate drift report is impossible by construction (vision has no future tense — anything future-tense is a bead). Drift is one-directional only. See CCP-wxv.

title: [CHORE] Bead-Label-Set: needs-adr, needs-helper, needs-enforcer-*, hoist-pending, allowlist-debt, pre-trinity-package, touches-contract
description:
Definiert und dokumentiert das Label-Set für Trinity-Workflow. Labels werden von architecture-scout, review-agent, allowlist-burndown und wave-orchestrator verwendet — brauchen konsistente Namen und Bedeutungen.

## Label-Set

- **touches-contract** — Bead verändert etwas, das einen Cross-Cutting-Contract berührt. Triggert bd lint --check=architecture-contracts
- **needs-adr** — Bead erzeugt oder deckt einen Contract auf, der kein ADR hat
- **needs-helper** — Ein shared Helper (typed primitive) fehlt noch
- **needs-enforcer-proactive** — Codegen/Builder fehlt
- **needs-enforcer-reactive** — Lint/Test fehlt
- **hoist-pending** — ADR hat hoist_when-Bedingung, die eingetreten ist (auto-gesetzt von /adr-gap)
- **allowlist-debt** — Bead adressiert einen Allowlist-Eintrag (auto-gesetzt von /allowlist-burndown --create-beads)
- **pre-trinity-package** — Ziel-Package hat keines der drei Artefakte für diesen Contract

## Doku

beads-workflow/skills/create/references/trinity-labels.md mit Definition, wann jedes Label gesetzt/entfernt wird, Auto-Erzeuger.

## Konvention

Beads mit needs-* Labels sind P1/P0 prioritärer als solche ohne. wave-orchestrator bevorzugt sie in Wave-Assembly.

## Refinements migrated from CCP-660 (Layer tagging, superseded 2026-04-19)

Add to the label set:

- **layer:<name>** — architectural layer the bead touches. Values configurable per project (no hard-coded enum). For mira-adapters: platform/application/agent/ig/catalog/pvs-adapter. For simple CLI projects: layers list may be absent entirely.

**Enforcement modes** (configurable in .claude/project-config.yml):

- `warn` (default): /create suggests a layer label; bead can be created without
- `hard-fail`: /create rejects bead creation without a valid layer:* label

Default warn so adoption isn't blocked. Projects opt into hard-fail when their layer model is mature.

**Tag inheritance**: child beads inherit parent's layer label by default. User can override at /create time. Same pattern beads already uses for other labels — keep consistency.

**Layers source**: read from .claude/project-config.yml `layers:` list, OR from docs/project-context.md Module Map → Layers subsection if config absent. No hard-coded values anywhere in harness code.

title: [FEATURE] /council --mode=architecture: Historian/Documentarian/Enforcer-Trio
description:
Erweitert business/skills/council um einen Architecture-Modus mit drei neuen Perspektiven, die speziell das Tripel-Denken beim Spec-/ADR-Writing vertreten.

## Drei neue Perspektiven

- **Historian**: liest bd search + open-brain timeline auf den Domain-Schlagworten. Fragt 'welche vergangenen Bug-Cluster verhindert / wiederholt dieses Dokument?'
- **Documentarian**: fragt 'welche impliziten Contracts müssen explizit werden? welche ADR fehlt?'
- **Enforcer**: fragt 'welcher Lint/Test/Codegen würde die Invariante nicht-umgehbar machen? ist das im Dokument festgelegt?' — mit Sub-Perspektive 'Codegen vs. Lint?' (proactive vs. reactive)

## Aktivierung

/council --mode=architecture (nur für ADR-Drafts, Specs, PRDs). Default-Modus bleibt End-User/Developer/Security/PO.

## Use Case

Besonders stark bei ADR-Drafts: ein ADR ohne konkreten Enforcer-Vorschlag ist unvollständig, und der Enforcer-Perspektiven-Agent sagt das.
design: Architecture-Modus läuft parallel zu den vier bestehenden Perspektiven (nicht Ersatz). Haiku-Subagents. Output: konsolidierte Tabelle sortiert nach Severity, wie bei Standard-Council.
acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] business/skills/council um --mode=architecture erweitert
- [ ] Drei Subagent-Prompts: historian, documentarian, enforcer
- [ ] Enforcer hat Sub-Frage: 'proactive (codegen) oder reactive (lint) bevorzugt?'
- [ ] Doku: references/architecture-council-prompts.md
- [ ] Integration-Test mit einem ADR-Draft als Input

title: [FIX] Remove || true from integration-check reference example (CCP-a81 follow-up)
description:
Follow-up from CCP-a81 iter-3 neutral review. The per-catalog query in beads-workflow/skills/wave-orchestrator/references/integration-verification-guide.md:177-180 uses:

  KBV_RESPONSE=$(curl -fsS --max-time 10 '...' 2>/dev/null) || true
  KBV_RC=$?

The '|| true' masks the curl exit code — $? is always 0 after this line, so KBV_RC is always 0 and the error branch at lines 181-185 is dead code. Transport/auth failures silently fall through to the JSON-parse path instead of emitting ERROR.

Fix: replace with either:
  KBV_RESPONSE=$(curl -fsS --max-time 10 '...' 2>/dev/null); KBV_RC=$?
  # (no || true; the subshell exit status is captured directly by $?)

Or:
  if ! KBV_RESPONSE=$(curl -fsS --max-time 10 '...' 2>/dev/null); then
    add_finding 'ERROR' 'infrastructure' ...
    STATUS='ERROR'
  else
    ...
  fi

Apply the same pattern to all per-catalog curls in the reference example.

title: [ARCH] Add catalog-keyword signals to arch-signal-detect.sh
description:
PRE_EXISTING finding from CCP-a81 Codex adversarial review.

Low-scoring catalog beads (score < 3) bypass both the arch-signal scorer AND the Haiku fallback, so a simple-looking bead importing KBV/DIMDI data can skip the Architecture Council entirely. Phase 6.5 integration-verification acts as a backstop but does not remove the upstream blind spot.

## Fix
Add catalog-specific keyword signals to scripts/arch-signal-detect.sh:
- STRONG signal (+3 pts) on: KBV|DIMDI|WHO|IFA|LOINC|SNOMED|ICD|Fachabteilungsschluessel
- STRONG signal (+3 pts) on: canonical.*catalog|external.*authorit
- OR force REVIEW_YES whenever bead description mentions these terms (regardless of total score)

This ensures any bead touching canonical reference catalogs gets architecture review, not just beads that happen to score >= 6 via other signals.

## Source
Discovered during CCP-a81 Codex adversarial review (phase 2). Not included in CCP-a81 scope per reviewer instruction.

description:
Current check in wave-status.sh line ~85-86:
```bash
if echo "$SCREEN" | grep -q "session-close"; then
  STATUS="session_close"
  DETAIL="Session close in progress"
fi
```

Matches literal string 'session-close' anywhere in the 60-line scrollback. False-matches when:
- bead-orchestrator prints 'next phase: session-close' or 'handoff to session-close' in Phase 3/4 recaps
- quick-fix HANDOFF message mentions 'session close' in past tense
- any phase description text references session-close as an upcoming step

Observed: CCP-a81 was in Phase 3 (Implementation via Codex rescue, 10m into composing) but wave-status reported status=session_close. Caused a T+10min false reading during monitoring of CCP-a81.

Fix idea: only match when session-close is the ACTIVE process. Check for:
- core:session-close agent invocation marker (⏺ core:session-close)
- 'Bash(git push' or similar merge/push tool-use markers near the bottom
- Or require the string 'session close' to appear in one of the last 5 lines, not the full 60-line scrollback

Priority: P3 — cosmetic monitoring error, no functional impact (BD status is authoritative).

title: [FEAT] Wave-2: Evaluierungs-Gate für Phase-2 (adversarial)
description:
## Ziel

Automatische Evaluierung ob Phase 2 (CMUX adversarial Review) noch Mehrwert liefert. Hinweis an User wenn Phase 2 konsistent keine CRITICAL Findings mehr produziert.

## Scope

### 1. Wave-Close Metriken-Auswertung

Nach jedem Wave-Close:
- Aggregiere phase2_findings und phase2_critical aus metrics.db für alle Beads der Wave
- Vergleiche mit Baseline (erste Waves nach Einführung der Model-Strategie)

### 2. 3-Waves-Gate

Wenn 3 Waves in Folge keine CRITICAL Findings aus Phase 2:
- Hinweis an User: "Phase 2 hat in den letzten 3 Waves keine CRITICAL Findings produziert. Überprüfen ob Phase 2 noch nötig ist?"
- NICHT automatisch deaktivieren — User entscheidet

### 3. ROI-Dashboard

Auswertungs-Script/Skill-Erweiterung für bead-metrics:
- Follow-up-Beads pro Wave (aus bd list mit parent-Referenz)
- Fix:Feat-Commit-Ratio pro Wave (aus git log)
- Phase-2 CRITICAL Findings Trend über Waves
- Model-Attribution: welches Model hat was gefunden

## Abhängig von

Wave-1 (metrics.db Erweiterung + Model-Routing) muss zuerst implementiert sein.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Nach Wave-Close: automatische Metriken-Zusammenfassung wird ausgegeben
- [ ] 3-Waves-Gate: Hinweis erscheint wenn Bedingung erfüllt
- [ ] bead-metrics Skill zeigt Wave-aggregierte Daten (phase2 findings trend)
- [ ] Follow-up-Beads und Fix:Feat-Ratio pro Wave querybar
